PROPOSAL.DESC: We are appalled at GA#416’s clarifying clause’s sweeping exemptions for capital punishment, euthanasia of the terminally ill (with no mandate that that euthanasia be voluntary), or acts of “insurgency” or “counterinsurgency” from ritual sacrifice that functionally makes the resolution useless and prevents the General Assembly from passing more accurate and constructive definitions of “ritual sacrifice” in future resolutions.
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Ban on Ritual Sacrifice"
Conserving Oceanic Reefs and Ledges

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The World Assembly,
Affirming that free trade and the movement of cargo to and from nations are vital to maintaining the stability of many national governments and the sustainability of national economies,
Lamenting the existence of GA#673 which purports to be about encouraging free trade, but due to its mandates, severely hinders the ability of nations to engage in the exchange and transportation of cargo,
Concerned that due to the definition of cargo, as shown in clause 1a being so vague as to result in any vessel that is capable of carrying anything that can be sold to be affected by this resolution, meaning that even personal fishing boats are required to be insured,
Condemning the inclusion of the WA Nautical Commission as the body responsible for handling an ill defined definition of produces for disputes, as it imposes unnecessary bureaucracy, prevents member states from resolving claims internally,
Finding that in Clause 5ai, the idea of a reputable insurer is vague and undefined, creating confusion over what counts as reputable and stifling competition, as smaller insurance firms are unable to demonstrate themselves to be as reputable as larger ones, resulting in monopolistic control of shipping insurance by just a handful of large corporations,
Alarmed at the number of paperwork that must be filed due to the inclusion of clause 5c, which forces large shipping firms to spend an absurd amount of time filing written reminders to cargo insurers greatly hindering the speed at which cargo can be moved between states,
Hereby repeals GA#673 "Maritime Cargo General Average"
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Maritime Cargo General Average"
Repeal "Don't Kill the Poor Act"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
GA 4 is clearly animated by a noble desire to think of all children. However, this resolution is both problematically written and has long been rendered obsolete by better crafted legislation. The continued existence of GA 4 is therefore an unconscionable oversight demanding immediate redress. It's essential for this body to resolve the negative consequences of its early efforts, particularly when those efforts are utterly redundant and create serious problems for an important topic.
Accordingly, the General Assembly finds as follows: [list=1][*]The target's policy focus, protecting children from trauma and harm in the workplace, has since been covered by [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=222/council=1]GA 222 "Prevention of Child Abuse,"[/url] which is both far better written and broader than the target while mirroring its key provisions regarding physical and psychological abuse as well as nonconsensual sexual acts. It additionally covers forcing minors into traumatic wartime service under "any deliberate act and/or behaviour which results in serious emotional and mental trauma in a child." Thus, GA 222 comprehensively renders the target obsolete. [*]Aside from the total redundancy with GA 222, the target also touches on a variety of subjects that have since been covered by this body in far greater detail: [list=a][*]The safety of employees, not just minors, in the workplace has been covered in greater specificity through resolutions such as [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=645/council=1]GA 645 "Responsible Handling of Toxic Materials."[/url] [*]This body has further legislated on the safety of children with resolutions such as [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=297/council=1]GA 297 "Child Welfare in Adoption"[/url] and [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=300/council=1]GA 300 "Child Pornography Ban."[/url] [*]Even going into the weeds of something as specific as sex work has been done by [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=672/council=1]GA 672 "Sex Worker Protections Act,"[/url] which includes a specific ban on the employment of those under the age of majority as sex workers.[/list] [*]In addition to this abundance of better written and more effective resolutions, the target contains several notable flaws: [list=a][*]Clause B6 bans the employment of minors in "work in environments that may expose them to hazardous substances, agents and/or processes." Given that even very effective safety measures can't completely eliminate the risk of accidents, this provision can interfere with the ability of minors to gain valuable work experience in settings such as labs or medical offices. [*]Clause B3 is similarly problematic, as work can involve "dangerous machinery, dangerous equipment or dangerous tools" without the worker running any reasonable risk of being harmed by such machinery, equipment, or tools. [*]Clause B7 banning the employment of minors in "work which would preclude the pursuit of a full-time education" is a needless restriction for those who have already completed their full-time education before reaching their nation's age of majority. At the same time, it's a very high bar to meet for those who may actually benefit from such a provision, since work could significantly interfere with someone's ability to pursue a full-time education without necessarily precluding it.[/list][/list] Satisfied that while the target may have served an important role in this venerable body's infancy, it has long since moved beyond and expanded from it for the above reasons, and
Wishing to put a venerable resolution to a long-overdue rest,
We repeal GA 4 "Restrictions on Child Labor."
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Restrictions on Child Labor"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
Helping people in need is a worthwhile goal, and well-written humanitarian aid resolutions such as [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=340/council=1]GA 340 "Access to Humanitarian Aid"[/url] and [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=334/council=1]GA 334 "Protected Status in Wartime"[/url] are a great way to effectuate that.
Unfortunately, a far older humanitarian aid resolution, GA 6 "Humanitarian Transport," is compromised by numerous irredeemable holes big enough to sail a humanitarian vessel through and has been comprehensively superseded by the far better written GA 340. Given the subject matter's importance and the target's utter redundancy, it's critical to repeal it posthaste.
Thus, the General Assembly finds as follows: [list=1][*]As mentioned, GA 340 meets the target's policy focus in a superior way. It protects the ability of humanitarian efforts to operate, handles potential misbehavior, shields them from undue interference, and even covers recognizable markings, and does so in a more encompassing way than the target. Repealing the target would cost this body nothing of value. This is especially the case given that the broader subject of conduct during wartime is also well-covered by GA 334. [*]The target is riddled with critical flaws: [list=a][*]It "[declares] the right of humanitarian vessels to maintain defensive weapons" without defining what actually qualifies as a defensive weapon. This potentially creates a gaping loophole for ferrying military equipment into conflict zones, contradicts the target's earlier discouragement from carrying weapons on humanitarian transports, and makes every humanitarian vessel a potential threat. [*]It "[authorizes] any national vessel within hailing distance of a humanitarian vessel to request, if there is sufficient cause, the humanitarian vessel to transmit their cargo manifest and prepare for boarding and inspection," and "[further requires] humanitarian vessels to present a cargo manifest and to submit to the above inspection." Without defining what constitutes "sufficient cause" or requiring communication between searching vessels, this leaves a loophole for nations to significantly impede humanitarian efforts through unnecessary searches. [*]It "[prohibits] searching vessels from firing upon any humanitarian vessel that has provided its cargo manifest, allowed a search, and surrendered any non-humanitarian cargoes" regardless of what actions the humanitarian vessel takes afterwards. This opens the drydock gates for rogue humanitarian vessels to attack searching vessels so long as they complied with the quoted provision, which is made even more hideously problematic by the aforementioned issue with the target not bothering to define or regulate "defensive weapons." [*]It prohibits "nations from firing upon vessels that are only carrying humanitarian cargoes" even though a nation would not know whether a given vessel is only carrying those cargoes until a search has been conducted. This provides belligerent nations with a dangerous degree of plausible deniability. [*]The target does not actually define a "humanitarian vessel" despite (vaguely) defining what constitutes "humanitarian cargoes." Additionally, clause 11 of the target implies that a vessel carrying non-humanitarian cargoes can still be considered a humanitarian vessel. This ambiguity complicates compliance even for nations operating in good faith, and is inexcusable where potentially life-saving relief efforts and potentially life-threatening situations are concerned.[/list][/list] Questioning why a redundant resolution that actively compromises an otherwise well-covered area of international law is still on the books, and
Vastly preferring GA 340's approach over a (bizarre) attempt to (poorly) legislate the specifics of boarding actions and naval engagements involving humanitarian vessels,
We repeal GA 6 "Humanitarian Transport."
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Humanitarian Transport"
Repeal "Ban Profits on Workers' Deaths"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
Determined to uphold the essential values of individual freedom and equal treatment across all member nations,
Acknowledging GA#299’s stated goal that “freedom to make important decisions about one’s life should not be unreasonably restricted”,
Nonetheless, concerned that the resolution in question fails to achieve this goal, as the standards set for when an individual’s personal freedom should be restricted go beyond the limit of what is reasonable, notably:
[list][*] The vague definition of “very low intelligence” in Article 2.1 allows governing bodies within a nation to oppress minority groups by declaring them legally incompetent according to a supposed standard of intelligence, which is especially worrying as there is no objective standard for intelligence or what constitutes “very low intelligence”, making it easy to manipulate this definition for malicious purposes;
[*] Article 2.2 specifies that the experts employed to measure compliance with these standards of intelligence must be “allowed by the authorities to act”, making it easy for said authorities to cherry-pick experts whose standards align with their own, even if the experts in question are acting fully autonomously as the target specifies;
[*] Article 4, when paired with the above definition and restrictions, allows governments to progressively set thresholds of legal allowance depending on intelligence, opening the door to various forms of discrimination;[/list]
Considering the resolution’s incompetence at achieving its stated goals,
The General Assembly repeals “Legal Competence”.
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Legal Competence"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The General Assembly,
Recognizing the importance of proper medical care and disease prevention considerations as a vital factor in the health of member states’ citizens,
However, worried that excessive and flawed international legislation on this topic will only serve to burden member states, preventing them from providing their residents with effective healthcare,
Dismayed at the fact that GA#492 assigns the duty of micromanaging standards of susceptible medical instruments (SMIs) to the Epidemic and Pandemic Alert and Response Center (EPARC), a clear overextension of the committee which was established by GA#53 for the purpose of establishing international protocol for responding to epidemics,
Concerned that making EPARC maintain standards for the handling of all different kinds of medical instruments found across all WA member nations poses an unnecessary and potentially overwhelming workload for the committee, preventing it from effectively focusing on the primary purpose it was established for,
Therefore, arguing that conventions on the handling of medical instruments would be better left to member nations to decide based on their own specifications, models and use cases,
Decidedly Repeals "Proper Hygiene For Medical Instruments".
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Proper Hygiene For Medical Instruments"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The General Assembly,
Convinced that collective punishment of civilian populations in wartime via reprisal violence is a despicable act that should be outlawed,
Relieved that [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=317/council=1]GA 317 "Wartime Looting and Pillage"[/url] already bans this reprisal violence,
Noting that this leaves GA 611 with the primary effect of banning collective punishment in non-wartime settings,
Sympathizing with the target in that collective punishment can be "grossly unfair" and "a serious violation of sapient rights,"
Saddened, therefore, that the target fails to effectively curb unjust domestic applications of collective punishment due to only banning actions by "member states" instead of outlawing such actions within members' jurisdiction, thereby leaving the door open for injustice so long as member states themselves are not the ones executing it,
Appalled at the hideous clumsiness of how the target's ban is worded, with its use of "purposefully punishing, convicting, or otherwise targetting [sic] for punishment" rather than specific forms of punishment such as "prosecution" causing it to overreach to the point of ensnaring legitimately beneficial actions taken by member states, such as sanctions and public safety measures, which cannot reasonably be divorced from member states themselves without grossly inhibiting their effectiveness,
Aghast, therefore, that the target's approach has the sum effect of rendering it unable to effectively prevent injustice it should cover while directing its practical effects against useful policies it absolutely should not,
Convinced that a resolution that so utterly undermines its own good intentions while crippling the tools available to governments acting in their peoples' interests should not remain on the books,
Pondering whether the approach of [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=323/council=1]GA 323 "No Penalty Without Law"[/url] indicates a better framework for protecting people from unjust penalties and discouraging collective punishment generally, and the approach of GA 317 is more effective at restricting specific forms of harmful collective punishment that warrant it, and
Beseeching this body to act against injustice itself rather than ineffectively banning an umbrella under which potential injustice may lurk,
Hereby repeals GA 611 "End Collective Punishment."
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "End Collective Punishment"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
Safeguarding sensitive animal populations and ecosystems is a noble endeavor, and GA 199 "Sustainable Fishing Act" represents a valiant effort to safeguard aquatic populations from needing to weather overharvesting. Unfortunately, the target has not itself weathered the passage of time and resolution.
Accordingly, the General Assembly finds as follows: [list=1][*]The target defines "Overfishing" as "Harvesting a population of an aquatic species in a manner that is impossible for that population to support over time, resulting in an overall decline in the health of the population and imperilling its long term viability." Numerous fishing crews, organizations, or governments operating independently could each be harvesting aquatic populations in a sustainable manner, thereby being compliant with the target even if their collective actions are grossly unsustainable. [*]The target exempts "actions taken to control Invasive Species" from its "restrictions on Overfishing." It defines "Invasive Species" as "A species that is not native to a particular ecosystem whose presence has proven disruptive or harmful to the local ecology." The lack of any test or provision specifying what form these actions can take renders the target pitifully weak. [list=a][*]Exempting any action from the target's overfishing restrictions so long as the action was taken to control invasive species creates a bewildering get-out-of-jail-free card for entities engaged in actions that should be prohibited under any reasonable understanding of the target's intentions. For example, enormously destructive sustained industrial trawling methods that are definitely impossible for local fish populations to support could fall under this exception if they are also undertaken to deplete and therefore control invasive species, regardless of how effective they are at actually controlling invasive species. [*]Any action taken with the purpose of invasive species control would fall under the exception regardless of how destructive or damaging it is to native aquatic populations or the environment itself. [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=725/council=1]GA 725 "Reducing Bycatch"[/url] wouldn't even apply here if those native populations are caught intentionally.[/list] [*]The target "[requires] that Member Nations determine and publish the Maximum Sustainable Yield of aquatic species populations inhabiting their national waters and any international waters over which they have an internationally-recognized jurisdictional right." This opens the door to these metrics being based on inaccurate, flawed, or biased data. [*]The target does not actually cap fishing at the "Maximum Sustainable Yield." This is a critical flaw that further kneecaps what meager protection the resolution does offer against overfishing and leaves the administrative burden on members to determine and publish "Maximum Sustainable Yield" functionally pointless, especially considering that the definition of "Overfishing" is based on "manner" of harvesting, which is fundamentally incongruent with "Maximum Sustainable Yield" simply meaning "maximum number of individuals." [*]This body has since passed better written protections for aquatic species threatened by sapient actions: [list=a][*][url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=465/council=1]GA 465 "Preventing Species Extinction"[/url] convincingly protects "all species and subspecies threatened with extinction by non-natural causes," including aquatic ones, and provides for recovery efforts. [*][url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=403/council=1]GA 403 "Trade of Endangered Organisms"[/url] more indirectly protects endangered species, including aquatic ones, by banning "the international import and export into or from member nations of all organisms from endangered species or subspecies, and of goods derived wholly or in part from said organisms," with actually reasonable exceptions. [*]The aforementioned GA 725 "Reducing Bycatch" cracks down on the potential harm of a specific fishing practice, as does [url=https://www.nationstates.net/page=WA_past_resolution/id=409/council=1]GA 409 "Ocean Noise Reduction."[/url][/list][/list] Emphasizing that the target exempts any actions taken to control invasive species from all of its overfishing restrictions, without bothering to consider the ecological damage that can result from overharvesting invasive species, or how easily this exception can be abused,
Understanding that invasive species themselves may be essential to the integrity of an ecosystem given how intricately interdependent ecosystems can be,
Bewildered that a resolution would decide that its stated mission of safeguarding "the long-term viability of aquatic populations" simply need not apply in such an appallingly clumsy way, and
Considering the target's crippling lack of meaningful protections especially given its redundancy with more recent resolutions to be both inexplicable and inexcusable,
We repeal the fishy target resolution.
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Sustainable Fishing Act"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The General Assembly,
Concerned by the target resolution's attempts to mandate that member states release any official government records or documents on request or voluntarily,
Acknowledging, nonetheless, that clause 3 contains a glaring flaw, which permits member states to restrict access to certain documents based on "legitimate and non-arbitrary criteria", an undefined exception allowing governments to restrict access to whatever documents they wish to hide,
Highlighting that clause 4, does not close this loophole, merely requesting that member states censor instead of block the release of restricted documents, which is non-binding,
Confused by clause 5's requirement to establish a vaguely defined "fair judicial appeals process", which can simply deny appeals for the same reasons given in clause 3, consequently creating unnecessary bureaucratic load,
Resolving that the aforementioned loopholes create a superfluous resolution, whose mandates are either non-binding, or may be entirely circumvented, thereby serving no purpose to justify remaining as extant legislation,
Hereby repeals GA#196, "Freedom of Information Act".
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Freedom of Information Act"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The General Assembly,
Recognising a responsibility to protect prisoners of war,
Desiring to comprehensively protect prisoners of war in member nations,
Hereby:
[list=1][*]Defines, for the purposes of this resolution, "prisoner of war", henceforth referred to as "POW", as any member of an armed service who has been captured by an opposing force.
[*]Bans the following practices: [list=a][*]The capture and internment of civilians by any military or nation, without reasonable suspicion of a crime or proof thereof, or unless there is a legal and substantiated warrant for arrest or a provable connection to an ongoing criminal proceeding, [*]The censorship of communication between POWs, and those they communicate to, where there is no demonstrable threat to security, [*]The application of corporal punishment, or any other form of torture, upon POWs, [*]The killing of POWs, [*]The coercion of information from POWs, and [*]The use of POWs for any form of forced labor.[/list]
[*]Requires that member nations provide the following to all POWs, regardless of any immutable distinguishing factor: [list=a][*]Sustenance and water sufficient to sustain life and healthy nutrition, [*]Facilities for living which are clean, adequately lit and heated, and protected from typical weather conditions, [*]Facilities and resources required to maintain basic hygiene, [*]Proper clothing, [*]Methods for periodical communication to their homeland, and [*]Any other facilities necessary to carry out the prior requirements.[/list]
[*]Allows member nations to engage in POW exchanges in wartime. All POWs in wartime exchanges must be safely transported to the point of exchange. Member nations should take necessary precautions to ensure their POWs' safety during transport as much as reasonably possible.
[*]Stipulates that all POWs must be released and returned upon the end of the respective war, except where continued detention is strictly necessary for a defined and lawful criminal process, and such detention shall be limited in duration to that which is reasonably required for investigation or adjudication.
[*]Stipulates that, if a POW is to die in captivity, that the remains are protected from desecration and properly returned to the family of the POW or the military they served, whichever is present.[/list]
PROPOSAL.NAME: Protecting Wartime Internees
On Basic Education

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The General Assembly,
Acknowledging its commitment to upholding the rule of law, preventing undue violence against citizens, and the promotion of peace and security among Member States and beyond,
Recognizing the potential consequences of the unregulated and unrestricted international trade of arms, such as human rights violations, the commission of terrorist acts, and the general degradation of peace and international goodwill,
Underlining the interests of member nations in certain international arms transfers of perfectly reasonable nature, and that the regulation of the international arms trade should not hamper international cooperation and exchange of materiel or other technology for peaceful purposes,
Emphasizing the exclusive and sovereign right of member nations to regulate and control arms transfers or related arms policies within their respective territory or territories,
Hereby:
[list=a][*]Defines the following terms for the purpose of this resolution: [list=i][*]“Arms” as equipment, technology, designs, or component parts for any of the previous that are likely or intended to be used to commit or support the deliberate application of lethal or destructive force in armed conflict, law enforcement, acts of terrorism, organized crime, or any military context; [*]“Transfer” as the intentional export, import, transit, shipment, digital transport, or brokerage of arms between member nations, from a member nation to a non-member nation, or from a member nation to a foreign sub-national group; [*]“Diversion” as the rerouting of an arms shipment from its authorized destination; and [*]“Transfer details” as any and all information regarding a particular shipment of arms, including but not limited to the: [list=1][*]Type of arms being transferred, as well as the original manufacturer, brand, and model; [*]The quantity of arms being transferred; [*]The method through which the arms are being transferred, as well as the estimated departure and arrival dates; [*]The intended final recipient of the shipment, as well as any intermediary nations, groups, or individuals; and [*]The intended use of the arms once received.[/list][/list] [*]Prohibits member nations from: [list=i][*]Authorizing the transfer of arms that are reasonably expected to lead to violations of active World Assembly legislation; and [*]Participating in any unauthorized transfer of arms.[/list] [*]Requires member nations to: [list=i][*]Positively and specifically authorize transfers of arms at their own discretion, following this resolution's guidelines, and to treat any arms that have not been specifically authorized for transfer by any nation as unauthorized; [*]Determine whether an arms transfer is authorized prior to participating in a transfer of arms, as the recipient or the sender; and [*]Take reasonable measures to prevent the unauthorized transfer of arms, as well as to prevent the diversion of arms transfers. [/list] [*]Urges member nations to ensure all relevant transfer details are promptly communicated or received to ensure that informed decisions on the authorization of arms transfers can be made, [*]Empowers the World Assembly Trade Commission to: [list=i][*]Serve as an intermediary for the purpose of collecting and disseminating transfer details, as well as decisions on the authorization of arms transfers between member nations; and [*]Investigate unauthorized arms transfers, as well as potentially fraudulent authorizations.[/list][/list]
PROPOSAL.NAME: Regulations on International Arms Transfers

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The World Assembly,
This regulation would ensure more protection for a nation’s national animal. This is vital for the population of the citizens in a nation to maintain happiness and feel that their government values what they want. It also increases the environmental protection of the nation's ecosystem by encouraging the protection of other animals and other parts of the ecosystem.
Regulations enforced by this proposal are listed:
Protections to the National Animal Itself:
1. National Animals cannot be used for sacrificial rituals involving the death of the animal.
(a. This includes a ban on removing body parts of the animal while still keeping it alive during ritual.
(b. No keeping a national animal in captivity for over ninety-six hours. For more, see under the category: Captivity and Placement of National Animal.
2. A National animal cannot be poached, killed, or mass murdered by any citizen or government official.
(a. If a national animal is tortured, this would give the torturer a criminal offence of poaching.
(b. A nation cannot make an order to change the national animal, kill a number of the previous national animal and then re-appoint the old national animal back. This would be considered poaching, mass murder, or killing.
3. Causing fear in the national animal voluntarily is a criminal offence.
(a. This includes fear caused by released chemicals in the environment.
Sidenote: This prohibition caused fear because that fear can drive away a national animal and cause health issues in a national animal because of stress.
Protection Regulations on a National Animal’s Habitat
1. Protection of a national animal’s habitat is advised but not mandatory, but it will improve the reputation of the nation if a member of the WA. Not mandatory, but advised!
2. Attacking a national animal within its habitat is a crime.
(a. This includes chemicals and toxins put into the environment.
Captivity and Placement of National Animal
1. A national animal cannot be held in confinement, such as a zoo, or any other confinement, for over ninety-six hours. A national official/organization/government member can not hold a national animal in confinement for any period of time.
Penalties if Caught Not Following this Policy
1. Penalties for not following this policy, if the offender is a leader of a nation, include:
(a. Removal of delegation if they are a WA delegate.
(b. Removal from the WA if the crime was over twenty national animals killed.
2. Penalties for not following this policy if the offender is a citizen of a nation in the WA include:
(a. Option of fees or jail time.
(b. Jail time is five months in prison per killed animal or per animal harmed/intentionally scared.
(c. Fees of five thousand units of a nation's currency per killed animal or per animal harmed/intentionally scared.
This proposal protects a part of a nation’s ecosystem and will encourage some nations to follow through with more laws helping the environment in their nations. This law promotes more environmental health in nations across the world.
Requires nations to protect their national animals based on the rules and regulations of this proposal. It requires nations to implement these new procedures to protect their national animal.
PROPOSAL.NAME: National Animal Protections

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The General Assembly,
Recognizing the good intent behind the aforementioned resolution,
Acknowledging that many of its provisions are fair and just,
Noting, however, that the restrictions against banning sexual activity between consenting individuals infringe on the right of states to restrict sexual activity to certain institutions, such as marriage,
Concerned that the restrictions against banning sexual activity based on the gender identities of the involved individuals infringe on the right of states and cultures to regulate such controversial topics,
Hereby repeals General Assembly Resolution #383 "Right to Sexual Privacy".
PROPOSAL.NAME: Repeal "Right to Sexual Privacy"

PROPOSAL.DESC:
The General Assembly,
Commendating the right of all citizens to strike to defend causes,
Respecting and wishing to preserve this right,
Knowing that there are public services that are indispensable for the care of the civilian population, without which many people can lose their lives, prevent access to services or put their safety at risk,
Defines "minimum services" as the percentage of the workforce of a sector that must go to work despite a call for strike, which must be alternated between the workforce to guarantee access to the strike for all people,
Hereby establishes minimum services for certain services that depend on the administrations of the member states, as detailed below:
[list=1][*]In public health:
[list=a][*]In primary care services, as well as Health Centers, Clinics, etc. in situations that do not entail an immediate risk to health, the full right to strike is permitted.
[*]In hospital and ambulance services, due to their importance in urgent and critical cases, a minimum service of 50% of the entire healthcare workforce is established, with the exception of external consultations, with a minimum service of 0%.[/list]
[*]In public education:
[list=a][*]The full right to strike is guaranteed at all educational stages.
[*]In the event that there are exams that are of relevant importance, such as university entrance exams or public service entrance exams, a minimum service of 100% is guaranteed solely for their implementation, correction and complaint.[/list]
[*]In public transport, a minimum service of 30% is guaranteed for all frequencies, with the aim of allowing people to travel effectively.
[*]In public centres that care for the elderly or minors, a minimum service of 50% will be provided.
[*]In security services, such as the police, army, firefighters and other rescue services, a minimum service of 50% of the workforce is established.
PROPOSAL.NAME: Minimal services During public protest acts

PROPOSAL.DESC: Nations have police,but [u]some [/u]criminals [i]can [/i]escape. We need police helicopters,motorcycles and more.Police and military budget is too low. So we need to raise it.
PROPOSAL.NAME: Police problems
